Designing for trust when you can't show a face

Aoife Shaw · 8 min read · Feb 12

Listen

Every design system I have ever shipped at a consumer product company has had an "avatars" component. Everywhere I have worked, that component has been the single most exhaustively documented piece of UI. We agonise over the fallback initials, the ring colour for active calls, the badge for verified users. We do this because we are taught from the first month of design school that faces build trust.

What do you do when you can't show a face? When the product is anonymous by design — a mental-health support app, a whistleblower platform, a peer-to-peer marketplace whose entire value proposition is that buyer and seller never meet?

The replacement isn't an icon

The mistake teams make is to substitute an avatar with a placeholder graphic — a coloured circle, a procedural blob, an LLM-generated abstract pattern. None of these build trust. They occupy the trust-bearing slot in the layout, and they communicate to the user that something is supposed to go there. But because the placeholder carries no signal, the user is left with a worse outcome than if the slot had been omitted entirely.

What I have learned, after building three of these products, is that the trust signal in face-less UIs has to come from elsewhere — and it has to come earlier in the flow.

Trust comes from receipts

The strongest face-less trust signal I have used is what I call a receipt: a small, specific, dated piece of information about the counterparty's previous behaviour. Not "1,247 positive ratings" — that's a number, and a number is a marketing pitch. A receipt is "responded within 3 hours, 14 of 14 times in the last month." It is concrete. It is dated. It is small enough to verify in your head.

The receipt does the work the face would have done. It says, "this is a real entity, doing real things, on a real schedule." And it does it without ever revealing who that entity is.

The dark pattern to avoid

The temptation in face-less UI is to lean on AI-generated content to fill the trust-bearing slots: synthetic testimonials, generated reviews, plausible-looking activity charts. Don't. These artefacts erode trust faster than they build it, and the moment the user catches one — and they will — the entire system is corrupted in their eyes. Receipts are slower to populate, but they are real.

Responses (12)
